title: Receiver operating characteristic (ROC) curve
body_html:
Definition & origin
Originating from radar signal detection during WWII, the ROC curve was later adopted in medicine to assess diagnostic test accuracy

It is a plot of sensitivity (true positive rate) on the y-axis versus false-positive rate (1 − specificity) on the x-axis for various test thresholds.


ROC curve. Click on image to enlarge

Purpose
The ROC curve helps visualise the trade-off between sensitivity and specificity as the diagnostic threshold changes

Used to:
  • Compare different diagnostic tests or models
  • Choose optimal thresholds that balance sensitivity and specificity

Area under the curve (AUC)
AUC summarises overall test performance:
  • 1.0 = perfect discrimination
  • 0.5 = no better than chance

Qualitative ranges:
  • 0.90–1.00 = excellent;
  • 0.80–0.90 = good;
  • 0.70–0.80 = fair

ROC curves. Click on image to enlarge

Optimal Threshold & Youden’s J
Youden's J statistic (also called Youden's index) is a single statistic that captures the performance of a dichotomous diagnostic test. The “best” cut-off point on the ROC curve maximises Youden’s index.

J = sensitivity + specificity − 1

It's chosen based on clinical context, weighing whether sensitivity or specificity is more critical. Graphically, it corresponds to the point on the ROC curve furthest from the diagonal line (the line of no discrimination i.e. AUC = 0.5)

Strengths
Provides a comprehensive evaluation of test performance across all thresholds.

Independent of prevalence, making it useful in studies with varied disease frequencies


Limitations & considerations
AUC may mask clinically important differences at specific thresholds, partial AUC (pAUC) may be more relevant in high-risk clinical regions

ROC curves do not reflect predictive values (i.e., PPV/NPV), which depend on disease prevalence .

Interpret AUC in the context of clinical consequences: e.g., poor sensitivity may matter more than poor specificity for some conditions

Key point
ROC curves and AUC are essential for evaluating and comparing diagnostic tests, especially when balancing sensitivity and specificity across different thresholds; however, interpretation must be rooted in clinical context and potential impact on patient care.
NCBI00
Receiver operating characteristic curve: overview and practical use for clinicians


BMJ00
Statistics Notes: Diagnostic tests 3: receiver operating characteristic plots
